data.title: CTA: I'm Upping My Exposure To Managed Futures (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I first started covering the Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:CTA) back in December, when I recommended it as a hedge against an uncertain S&P 500. CTA is a managed futures fund, meaning that it trades derivatives contracts across a variety of assets, including equities, bonds, currencies, and commodities with the goal of producing positive absolute returns in all market conditions. The reason one may include it as a hedge to an equities or bond portfolio is because it can provide positive returns in years that are bad for equities or bonds in particular, and provide an expectation of consistent positive returns. This doesn't always play out, but that's the theory behind it.

Managed futures funds have become very popular in the past few years, as savvy ETF managers have become eager to bring these kinds of "hedge fund like" tools to retail traders. What the fund is doing is actually run by a different kind of investment company than a hedge fund, called "commodities trading advisers," which is where we get the ticker CTA for Simplify's fund.

Since my first recommendation in December, it's done very well, not just as a hedge, but even as markets have reached higher and higher. Here is its performance since inception against the market, bonds, gold, and a trend-following fund (the passive version of managed futures), KMLM.

CTA has absolutely smoked most of these assets, which is impressive considering these managed futures funds are often touted as funds that should only really shine in down or sideways markets. The folks at Simplify, or more specifically the sub-adviser to the fund, Altis Partners, have really proven their chops at identifying trends and taking strategic long/short positions to capture them regardless of the market conditions.

To this end, I have also included this fund in my multi-asset portfolio, which I currently have a significant portion of my personal portfolio in.

CTA Holdings

The fund repositions itself across its various markets constantly, meaning that the fund could take new positions at will, and does on a day-to-day basis. This turnover, and the associated black box that it takes place in, can be a red flag for some investors. I do not mind active management, and believe that it can lead to outperformance, but there are folks that disagree with me.

While we can look at CTA's holdings, it's nearly impossible to speculate on why they are holding any given position. This is due to the decision-making of the management not being public knowledge, and the nature of futures making it unclear if any positions are linked to each other as pairs, or how long they intend to hold on to any given position.

This is something that investors need to be at peace with before considering an investment into a managed futures ETF, especially actively managed ones like CTA and the iMGP DBi Managed Futures Strategy ETF (DBMF).

The actual positions take several screenshots to post because they are spread across an absolute ton of different futures contracts, and I don't want to make them so small that they're impossible to read in one screenshot.

If you're interested in going through the holdings, just know that they may have already changed by the time this article is published, as they can change daily and without any notice. You can find current ones updated daily on Simplify's website.

To help folks understand these holdings a bit more clearly, Simplify has also listed out their exposures to given assets. Those holdings translate to these weights, essentially, for each instrument. I am very glad that they provide this view of the fund, as it gives a lot of insight into the fund's real weightings toward each instrument, and how much they contribute to the fund's volatility.

Note that "total" is in the middle of the table, since the net weight of the fund is usually flat to slightly negative. It's currently at (1.49%).

The fund itself is very heavy on commodities and short treasuries. While we can't know exactly what the fund manager is thinking, we can get a sense of it through this view of the fund's holdings.

Also note the heavy long exposure to gold, coffee, and natural gas, which may explain some recent gains made by the fund as these commodities have done very well over the last few months. CTA is also considerably short the 5yr and 2yr UST, indicating that the managers believe that yields may continue to rise.

On a personal note, I also believe that there may be more pain in store for the 2yr and 5yr bonds. If you want to read more commentary about it, I discuss it in my report on the January CPI numbers.

The recent jobs report also caused yields to rise, as the bond market seems to be re-adjusting to the reality of returning inflation and low unemployment. I also wrote about that this month.

It seems to me that CTA is positioned to be long almost all commodities, as we see inflation push prices up, leading to the Fed pushing rates up (and treasuries down), cause the price of commodities across the board to rise.

That's about as much as I can comment on their positioning, as the rest of it is in commodities that I am not familiar enough with to comment on why they may be a good position or not. Apologies to those who wanted more commentary on why they have over a 10% allocation to corn. That's just part of the black box that Altis Partners operates within.

CTA's 10% Yield

The main attraction for a lot of folks when it comes to these funds, outside the diversification reason, is that they can have high yields. Simplify has CTA's distribution rate at 10.62%, as of the end of last year.

Simplify has been accused of "running experiments with their live funds," by commenters on my articles in the past, and I understand why. The dividends were all over the place in the past.

Looking at the most recent distributions, and we see a very odd quarterly schedule, with the only actual payout so far being the December one. The rest are listed as $0.00 paid, but are still counted as distributions.

I would expect CTA to only pay out annually, moving forward. Beware that this yield is not a promise, and may not stay this high or happen at all in the future.

Suitability

CTA's performance, and its manager's keen eye for spotting trends and taking advantage of them, have given me more trust for them. Currently, CTA occupies a 10% allocation in my multi-asset portfolio. I am considering raising that allocation to 15%.

Today, I am raising my recommendation to investors. Aggressive investors could consider up to a 20% allocation to CTA as a diversifying element to an equities, income, or hybrid portfolio. While income is a weak spot for CTA since it doesn't seem to have consistency behind it, and it may only pay annually, it still carries a trailing distribution rate of 10%, making it an effective income instrument in the past.

In the past, I have recommended pairing CTA with another manager, to diversify key-person risk, e.g. the risk that the manager of the fund makes the wrong move. I still recommend pairing CTA with DBMF.

Moderate investors could consider up to a 10% allocation to CTA, or a 15% total allocation to managed futures, with a split between CTA and DBMF.

Conservative investors could consider up to a 5% allocation to managed futures, and should consider KMLM instead of CTA or DBMF to avoid active management risk altogether.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I am bullish on the Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF because of the past performance of active manager Altis Partners, the sub-adviser to CTA that picks and chooses its trades. They successfully have traded up along the trends of several big commodities swings in the last few months, including gold, natural gas, and coffee. They are also trading short treasuries, which, I believe, is strategic for our current economic conditions. The fund has successfully served as an example of what active managers worth their salt can accomplish. I expect this kind of insight and performance to continue from them moving forward, regardless of equity market conditions.

Because of the management and their black box strategy being so integral to returns, I am recommending that investors pair CTA up with competing managed futures ETFs, and particularly the iMGP DBi Managed Futures Strategy ETF.

I am considering increasing my exposure to managed futures as a whole, and particularly to CTA, when I update my personal portfolio in the coming weeks as we head toward the end of Q1.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Dutch Bros: Brewing A Legacy - 1,000 Stores And Counting
data.description:
Hot Coffee, Hotter Results

Who needs sugar when Dutch Bros’ (NYSE:BROS) earnings are this sweet?

The coffee chain that now spans the United States from coast to coast reported another stronger-than-expected quarter, beating both forecasts for top-line sales by $24 million and EPS by $0.05. And what’s more, Dutch Bros just hit its 1,000-location milestone nationwide!

But let's take it one step at a time. We have a lot to talk about today, but don't rush! Grab a coffee and enjoy!

First things first. We saw another double-digit growth in top-line sales. They were approximately $342.8 million during Q4, growing approximately 34.9% year-over-year. This was driven by the addition of 151 new locations, as well as a tailwind of same-store sales growing 6.9% year-over-year.

Breaking down a bit of Dutch Bros same-store sales for this quarter, we can see that there was a 2.3% year-over-year increase in transactions and an average check per guest growth of 4.6% year-over-year. Both accelerated from last quarter, showing clear sequential improvements.

Surely this doesn’t just happen, does it? To achieve these sequential improvements, Dutch Bros has relied on its mobile ordering (remember when it was all a test in Arizona?), marketing efforts and maturation in certain markets like Tennessee, New Mexico and Oklahoma, as well as late-penetration markets like Florida.

Of course, a quarter of fewer discounts and higher prices also created tailwinds for the average check per guest.

Now take a look at how this played out when we look at the bigger picture. Here I've compiled annual sales, same-store sales and asset turnover data for Dutch Bros:

Note that from the moment Dutch Bros decided to grow almost exclusively the company-owned units segment, the asset turnover began to transition as the mix of corporate properties increased within the company's portfolio. The reason is clear as day, isn't it? Precisely to maintain cohesion in the value proposition and training of Broistas as they advance state-by-state.

These company-operated stores saw higher same-store sales during FY 2024. They grew 9.5% year-over-year during Q4 FY 2024, as well as saw traffic grow 5.2% year-over-year. Simply incredible!

To keep the pipeline hot as a freshly brewed coffee, Dutch Bros is aiming to see double-digit year-over-year growth in its locations. During FY 2024, they saw their location count grow 18% year-over-year, adding 151 locations. For FY 2025, they are set to add around 160 new stores, aiming to reach 1,142 locations by the end of the year. That’s 16.3% year-over-year growth, a slowdown from last year, but still stronger than almost every other restaurant/coffee chain out there.

To assess Dutch Bros’ sustainable growth and competitive advantage with the more economical boxes, we need to look inside them. In fact, in my last article I only briefly touched on this issue. Let’s delve a little deeper now.

We know that each company-operated location has the following characteristics:

  1. Construction costs around $1.8 million. Of course, Dutch Bros has plans to reduce this drastically by FY 2025 and facilitate penetration into markets with smaller DMAs, generating a more interesting ROI in medium-low volume situations. We will look at this possibility later;
  2. AUVs of approximately $2 million; and
  3. Restaurant-level margins of 29.7%. Note that they have grown significantly compared to what they were during FY 2023 (28.2%).

I want you to see for yourself how economical these boxes are:

  • Assuming an average cost of capital of 7.5% and a perpetual growth rate of 2.5%, the PV of a Dutch Bros location amounts to $10,293,483.04 (growing 5.3% year-over-year from the improvement in restaurant-level margins) and an NPV of $8,493,483.04;
  • It has a payback period and discounted payback of 4 years;
  • The Benefit-Cost Ratio is 5.72 (in FY 2023 it was 5.43). For those who don't know, this is the ratio between the sum of the projected cash flows for a location discounted to present value and the construction cost. This means that the sum of these discounted cash flows is 5.72x the costs;
  • The IRR of the construction project is 31.7%. Note that during FY 2023 it was 30.1%.

These are great numbers, but they may not be suitable for locations with weak DMAs. Well, Dutch Bros could do the same as Portillo's (PTLO) and launch leaner locations to increase penetration, which is already quite attractive. One of the keys is to reduce the payback period. To do this, Dutch Bros needs to focus on reducing its construction costs and preserving its margins.

Let's assume that Dutch Bros manages to reduce construction costs by at least 20% for FY 2025. What would be the impact on unit-level economics metrics? Here's the thread:

  • Considering construction costs of $1.44 million, we achieve a three-year payback period, as well as a discounted payback period of the same length;
  • A Cost-Benefit Ratio of 7.15, increasing at least 25% compared to what Dutch currently delivers;
  • An impressive 40% IRR.

An economic analysis doesn't mean much when we look at the industry, does it? Below I compared the data from Dutch Bros' unit projects and compared it with other restaurant chains:

Note that despite being in the top quartile when it comes to unit savings, Dutch Bros has room to improve when compared to trendy fast-casuals like Wingstop (WING) and Sweetgreen (SG), which can deliver payback periods of less than three years! It is indeed possible to achieve this. All it would take is a double-digit reduction in construction costs.

Renewed Real Estate Strategy: What Does This Mean in Practice?

But it appears that there are other plans in place alongside the prototypical developments. Dutch Bros has just entered FY 2025 with a revamped real estate strategy.

What does this mean in practice?

The first difference is the lease models. For most of its history, Dutch Bros used Ground Leases. These construction models are relatively more expensive and complicated, as Dutch only leased the land and took on the responsibility of setting up and maintaining the property. This made the average construction cost for a drive-thru location $1.9 million.

Under the new strategy, Dutch Bros has adopted Build-to-Suit Leases, where the developer builds the property to the tenant’s specifications. This improves both site selection and de-risks construction and development. This has saved the company at least $100,000, which is now working with construction costs of $1.8 million per location.

The second difference is that they have completely abandoned the Rapid Densification Strategy, as we saw in Texas. They opened about two hundred locations there in less than three years! This has led to some problems related to cannibalization, as we have seen in other concepts, especially FSRs like Kura Sushi (KRUS) in the same state.

To get around this, the drive-thru coffee shop chain will not have as its main objective rapid penetration, but rather to strengthen the brand image, investing in advertising to generate brand awareness before going all out with the openings. This is already underway in its recent expansion in Florida!

But the most interesting thing of all is that Dutch recently announced that this new strategy has increased store-level productivity. Just look at the traffic growth and you will see that the overlap has decreased a lot.

Mobile Ordering, Innovation, and Food: How Dutch Bros Plans to Keep Driving Traffic

It is worth remembering that Dutch Bros maintains its transaction generation strategy based on three fronts. They are:

  1. Menu innovation;
  2. Increased paid advertising; and
  3. Focus on Dutch Rewards.

Of course, we can consider the mobile ordering channel as one of these initiatives.

For menu innovation, Dutch Bros brought back fan-favorites Hazelnut Truffle Mocha and Candy Cane Mocha for the holiday season. They also added a new drink, Winter Shimmer Rebel. And in early 2025, for Valentine’s Day, they’re launching the Dutch Luv duo, Chocolate Covered Strawberry Mocha and Strawberries & Cream Rebel.

Note that this mix of seasonal items, especially established LTOs, creates tailwinds for same-store sales from increased transaction volume.

Speaking of paid advertising, Dutch Bros is using it not only in late-penetration markets, but also in established markets. Management said that during the second half of 2024 brand awareness in mature markets has increased even further!

Now, talking about Dutch Rewards, which accounts for about 71% of the company’s total sales in FY 2024, or $909.5 million (last year it accounted for 66% of Dutch Bros’ total sales, or approximately $637.4 million), it really was a success! Note that this number has already surpassed Starbucks Rewards' numbers, which had 41% of its sales within the United States indexed to app members during FY 2024.

Rewards members made approximately 5.5 million transactions through mobile ordering throughout FY 2024! Keep in mind that this only started in mid-2024, which just shows the potential of these two platforms together.

But there is one more potential traffic lever that I already warned you about during my last article: Food!

Management said early results are encouraging, with food still accounting for 2% of total Q4 sales (about $6.9 million). In fact, they also said they are losing morning beverage transactions from potential customers who are not satisfied with our current food offerings. Now, the initial test has very few items, so they are generating incremental dollars from guests who visit the drive-thru to purchase beverages.

It turns out that as they expand the offerings, selecting a mix of desirable foods, they will be able to generate incremental beverage sales from guests who are looking for food, especially during breakfast. Keep in mind that this is a guest who is still over-indexed on traditional fast food, especially Wendy's (WEN), and is looking for a cheap meal before work. Once they get that, they will be on to a gold mine.

Keeping an Eye on Finances

As we’ve seen, top-line sales increased 34.9% year-over-year during Q4 and 32.6% year-over-year for FY 2024. This marks Dutch Bros moving into a company with over $1 billion in annual sales!

Looking at cost centers, Food, Beverage and Packaging accounted for 25.4% of total revenue in the fourth quarter (about $87.1 million). They improved 120 points year-over-year, taking Dutch Bros to at least 460 percentage points below the '30/30/30 Rule' benchmark.

Something threatens the integrity of this result for FY 2025. The price of coffee has soared more than 119% over the past year! As of the day this article is being written, the rally appears to have lost steam, but it is still trading above $4.10. If this scenario persists, Dutch Bros expects COGS to fall 110 basis points over FY 2025.

That is, if the price of coffee remains above the $4 level for an extended period of time, Food, Beverage and Packaging costs will be approximately 26.5% of revenue (approximately $417 million for the entire FY 2025), reversing part of the advances the company achieved during FY 2024. Note that although this impacts the P&L, it is not something disastrous as the bears sell.

Labor Costs and Overheads remain in the range of 27.1% and 17.5% of total revenue respectively.

These improvements have benefited margins. See how they have behaved over the past few years in the following chart:

Note that all margins saw benefits from sales leverage, successful SG&A leverage (which reduced its proportionality to revenue by at least 400 basis points year-on-year), reduction in debt costs and cost containment.

This is especially important as Dutch Bros is increasingly becoming a self-funding company. Note that during FY 2024, the company generated a record OCF of $246.4 million, exceeding Capex and generating FCF for the first time since it transitioned from a coffee shop chain expanding through franchising to company-operated stores! This is quite unusual when we look at growth stocks. They often sacrifice profitability in order to grow, but that doesn't seem to be the case here.

By generating FCF and not paying dividends, Dutch Bros no longer needs to issue as much short-term debt as it used to. Remember that this was one of the central themes of my first article about the company? How time flies!

Dutch Bros secured long-term financing at the beginning of the year, which allows them to maintain a very satisfactory level of liquidity. They have both a positive NWC of $154 million and $293.4 million in cash on hand. This is a very cautious position to deal with a possible increase in operating asset accounts, which gives them great flexibility to negotiate terms without depending on their operating liabilities.

You could argue that this level of liquidity is unnecessary, and I would agree. But we can’t afford to play around with the Scissors Effect, especially in a company that is growing as fast as Dutch Bros At the end of the day, they only have $217 million in long-term debt, which implies a Debt/EBITDA ratio of 0.99x, which is almost risk-free. Add in the cash on hand and you’ll see that analyzing their debt sustainability is pretty much irrelevant here.

Rome wasn't built in a day. Understanding how Dutch Bros changed its expansion model for corporate stores and the impacts of this on its cash flow is very interesting. Note that in 2021, when the company started investing more in opening its drive-thrus and went public, the OCF generation was not enough to meet such a high investment need. Time passed and here we are. FY 2024 will be remembered as the year in which Dutch Bros became a self-financing company!

Looking ahead, management released the following guidance for FY 2025:

  • Total revenues are projected to be between $1.555 billion and $1.575 billion. This represents approximately 21% to 23% year-over-year growth;
  • Open at least 160 new stores, representing a system growth of 16%;
  • Same-store sales growth for the system is estimated at 2% to 4% for the full year;
  • Capital expenditures are estimated to be in the range of $240 million to $260 million, comprised primarily of new store construction costs;
  • Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be between $265 million and $275 million, representing 15% to 20% year-over-year growth.

Valuation: How to Price This Growth?

It's both interesting and funny how many analysts try to value fast-growing companies. Don't get me wrong, I include myself here. It's a daunting task!

Nights change, tables turn, and many growth stocks are being looked at less fondly than they once were. Wingstop, Sweetgreen, and CAVA (CAVA) are classic examples. But Dutch Bros has something different. It really could be America's next favorite.

When comparing it to other coffee chains like Starbucks (SBUX), we can't use the same yardstick. Dutch Bros is a fast-growing stock, so it's better to compare it to trendy fast-casuals. That way we can see how much the market would be willing to pay for that growth.

I've selected almost all of the hottest fast-casual restaurants in the world right now, including Shake Shack (SHAK), Sweetgreen, CAVA, Wingstop, and Portillo's.

Our mindset here is to define the size of the premium that investors are willing to pay for the growth of these restaurants and try to find a target price for Dutch Bros based on that. Now, if they're paying that premium for those stocks, why not for this coffee shop that's rocking it?

Just one more caveat. I used sales multiples, as they are the most appropriate for valuing fast-growing companies. If we were to use P/E or multiples such as EV/EBITDA or EV/EBIT, we could bias the valuation since these companies are at different stages of their growth. An example is Sweetgreen, which does not yet generate net income, or even Portillo's, which already generates strong operating cash flow, as well as Dutch Bros There are many facets that we need to look at here, it is easier to use a more general approach.

The result is interesting, and may tell us that there is more upside to come. Take a look at this:

  1. The average P/S of comparable companies is 7.87. Considering top-line sales of $1.555 billion, in line with the lower guidance estimates for FY 2025, we have an RPS of $11.92. This would give us a PT of $93.83. Now using top-line sales of $1.575 billion, we arrive at an RPS of $12.07, which implies a PT of approximately $95.02. The average of these two RPS estimates gives us a PT of$94.42;
  2. The average EV/S of comparable companies is 8.89. Considering top-line sales of $1.555 billion and the company's current capital structure, we have a PT of $100.94. Similarly, when we use top-line sales of $1.575, we have a PT of $102.30. Averaging the two gives us a PT of$101.62.

Note that this gives us a PT of around$98-$100 based on revenue estimates for Dutch Bros in FY 2025. They may beat those estimates later on, but it seems that based on the valuation of other restaurant chains out there, Dutch Bros could still have at least 17% upside.

So in my opinion, the rally is not likely to stop now. I reiterate my 'Buy' rating here, very bullish on this coffee chain that is getting everything right that Starbucks is getting wrong.

data.title: Allspring Emerging Markets Equity Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The MSCI EM Index (Net) declined 8.01% in the quarter as the index fell sharply in October and November.

U.S. elections, uncertain trade policy, the strong dollar, China’s struggling economy, and domestic political challenges in emerging markets were market drivers. In October, the U.S. election weighed on risk assets, Indian equities fell as growth slowed, and South Africa performed strongly. In November, Trump’s electoral victory raised the specter of protectionist trade policies and greater geopolitical tension, and China and exporters lagged. In December, emerging markets outperformed the S&P 500 Index and the MSCI EAFE Index (Net), led by Taiwan technology stocks, Gulf countries that benefited from their currency pegs to the U.S. dollar, and China, while Korea dealt with a political crisis.

Emerging market currencies fell 3.59% versus the U.S. dollar, as the U.S. Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) “hawkish cut” and a surging U.S. dollar took the wind out of the sails of rate-sensitive countries. Twenty-one of 24 emerging market currencies fell, and the currencies of Hungary and Korea declined the most.

Performance in the MSCI EM Index was broadly weak as 10 of 11 sectors and 20 of 24 countries fell. The largest outperformers included information technology, financials, UAE, Taiwan, and Kuwait, while laggards included materials, consumer discretionary, Brazil, Korea, and Indonesia.

The fund underperformed its benchmark for the trailing 12-month period. Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

2025 is shaping up to be a volatile year for assets globally, but we continue to find attractive opportunities in emerging markets at the country, sector, and company level. The incoming U.S. government has proposed tariffs of 10% to 60% on some emerging market countries, setting the stage for a global trade war. However, some tariff proposals may be intended for use as bargaining chips to gain concessions on other issues. Further, emerging economies have been adjusting to tariff threats for several years. It seems likely to us that new tariffs may have less impact than expected. The U.S. dollar has strengthened since the November election, but the greenback has become extremely expensive by many measures. Should the U.S. dollar slow its ascent, it would be a positive surprise for emerging market assets. We believe that emerging market companies are in better shape than markets appear to acknowledge and that companies are optimistic about the future. In fact, Purchasing Managers’ Index data in most emerging market countries looks much healthier than in the U.S. or Europe. Finally, the valuation discount between emerging and developing markets has continued to widen, and valuations in the developed world are stretched. We think 2025 may be more positive for emerging market equities than investors expect.

data.title: Higher Fee Revenues, Brighter Outlook Boost Mood Of Private Equity's Big 4
data.description:
Record revenues from management fees and a brighter outlook for M&A in the year ahead helped private equity's Big Four close 2024 on a high note.

The average net positivity score for the four largest listed alternative asset managers - Apollo Global Management Inc. (APO), Blackstone Inc. (BX), The Carlyle Group Inc. (CG) and KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) - rose to its highest level in several years, according to an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis of the language used by executives and analysts on fourth-quarter 2024 earnings calls.

Strong fee-related earnings (FRE), a key measure of asset manager performance and profitability, gave executives something to crow about. Carlyle and KKR set annual records for FRE in 2024, while both Apollo and Blackstone reported their best-ever quarterly FRE marks in the fourth quarter.

The executives' 2025 outlook also leaned positive, with many predicting higher M&A levels as cheaper financing and pent-up demand drive transaction activity. Blackstone CFO Michael Chae said this should include a healthy dose of private equity exits, whose scarcity in recent years has hindered fund managers' ability to raise money from investors.

"We see a much more constructive environment for realizations in 2025," Chae said.

AUM update

The Big Four firms all reported higher FRE in 2024 compared to 2023. KKR's FRE grew the most year over year, increasing 37% to $3.27 billion, or $3.66 per share, in 2024.

KKR also ran just behind Apollo on two key growth metrics. The firm increased total assets under management 15.3% year-over-year to $637.53 billion by the end of the fourth quarter, and raised the portion of AUM that generates management fees 14.7% to $512 billion over the same period. Apollo's year-end 2024 totals of $751.04 billion in AUM and $568.7 billion in fee-earning AUM were both up 15.4% from the fourth quarter of 2023.

Meanwhile, Carlyle lagged its peers on both metrics. The firm grew AUM just 3.5% year-over-year in 2024 while the portion of AUM that generates fees shrank nearly 1% over the same period.

Carlyle CFO John Redett put a positive spin on the decline in the firm's fee-earning AUM, noting that exits from private equity investments factored into the drop.

"Realizations are good in our business in the sense we're giving capital back to our [limited partners (LPs)]. Our LPs like to see capital return," Redett said.

Credit drives fundraising

Private credit strategies continue to be a top priority for the Big Four's LPs and are the main driver of their fundraising.

Blackstone, the largest of the four by AUM, also led its peer group in fundraising, reporting a $171.5 billion haul in 2024. Of that total, 53%, or $91.2 billion, flowed to the firm's private credit strategies, while Blackstone's various private equity strategies attracted $41.3 billion, or about 24% of all capital committed to the firm in 2024.

Fundraising was even more lopsided at Apollo. The firm's private credit strategies drew $142.6 billion in new commitments in 2024, accounting for nearly 94% of the $152.4 billion raised by the firm in 2023.

"Credit is simply more attractive in absolute terms in the environment we're in," Apollo CEO Marc Rowan said.

Total return performance

Apollo's stock turned in the best performance between the start of 2024 and the end of the fourth-quarter 2024 earnings season in mid-February 2025, delivering a 71.5% total return to investors over that period, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. KKR followed with a total return of 69.5%.

Total returns of 27.1% for Blackstone and 26.7% for Carlyle slightly lagged the S&P 500, which produced total returns of 28.8% over that period.

Dividend forecast

Blackstone's dividend of $1.44 per share was the highest among the four firms in the fourth quarter. That dividend is expected to fall in 2025, according to Eclipse, an S&P Global dividend forecasting service.

Recently updated forecasts for Apollo and KKR predict both will continue with annual dividend increases, citing the firms' reported growth in fee revenue.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: GLD: The Trump Factor In Gold's Rally
data.description:
The SPDR Gold Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:GLD), launched on 11/18/2004 and managed by World Gold Trust Services, LLC, is an ETF that tracks the performance of gold bullion. GLD manages $81.85B and charges an expense ratio of 0.40%. As the biggest ETF that offers exposure to the price of gold, it provides very high liquidity based on its trading volume.

There is a case to be made about having exposure through such an efficient way in the long term. But in this article, I want to examine the recent trend of gold's price relative to other assets, explore the potential factors that resulted in the rally, and assess the sustainability of the current trend by understanding how impactful such factors will continue to be based on what we know so far.

Long story short, I believe that the recent demand has more room to grow and a tilt towards gold or a short-term trade is warranted. You should, however, read the whole article to understand the drivers and, therefore, the risks of underperformance.

Disclaimer: In this article, I am not concerned about the long-term effects of the current administration's policies on the U.S. and global economy. This article doesn't discuss this matter as it's completely irrelevant for traders and investors looking to formulate an outlook for the next few months. The markets are neutral for the most part because profit is the end goal. In case my tone doesn't make it clear enough as you go through this article, keep in mind that I strive to have the same stance when I write such articles that involve monetary, fiscal, or trade policy.

Let's take things from the start.

What Is Happening To the Price of Gold?

Gold is back in vogue. And that may be thanks to U.S. President Donald Trump's proposed tariff policies that could support the current inflation trend and ignite a global trade war.

This also offers an explanation of why other asset classes have performed so poorly; not just relative to gold but on an absolute basis during a time when inflation hovers around 3%.

YTD, gold holds the title as one of the best-performing assets since it has increased by almost 12%. In comparison, the U.S. dollar has gone down relative to a basket of major currencies and the 10-year Treasury price has had a modest increase; despite the safe haven status of 10-year Treasuries, it can't do much when inflation and interest rate expectations shift upwards so fast. The S&P 500 and even Bitcoin have been significantly underperforming gold lately as well, indicating risk appetite is not as strong as in the recent past.

This phenomenon is a reflection of the hedge quality that gold enjoys during times of expectations for higher geopolitical tensions and market volatility.

The Israel-Hamas conflict has de-escalated but is not resolved yet. The Russia-Ukraine peace agreement is yet to come. And a potential trade war as a result of new tariffs on imported goods in the U.S. further fuels uncertainty. Such a scenario could trigger higher upward pressure on inflation and introduce a challenge to economic growth.

The recent gold shipments from the Bank of England's vaults to New York also display an interesting correlation as the higher price of gold is accompanied by New York inventories surging this year.

As a result of tariff fears, an arbitrage opportunity emerged for major players like JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and HSBC (HSBC); the price spread between London Bullion and Comex widened after Trump announced his intention to introduce tariffs on aluminum and steel imports. According to Comex filings, JPMorgan is going to deliver $4 billion of gold in February, reflecting the impact higher demand for gold has right now on the momentum.

As long as the tariff risks are not resolved, gold should remain one of the most attractive assets among traders and investors. Yahoo Finance UK readers were asked about their expectations and 60% believed that gold could reach $3,500 in 2025.

The Case for Gold Now

So, the question for any investor who missed the action right now is how those tariffs are going to play out. First of all, you have to understand that the effects of tariffs on inflation are debated. The honest answer is that we just don't know how this will play out (but there's good reason to believe that the uncertainty will drag out; more in a bit). One Fed official believes that the tariffs will not result in much higher prices and that the impact on inflation will not persist. Others prefer to err on the side of caution and assume that the effects will last longer.

Economic growth may also take a hit; The Tax Foundation projects that the Canada/Mexico tariffs could decrease GDP growth by as much as 0.3% while tariffs on China could reduce it by 0.1%. While it estimates that steel/aluminum tariffs will have a much more modest impact (less than a 0.05% decrease), we still don't know how the so-called reciprocal tariffs scheduled to take effect in April will play out.

At the same time, the tariffs could introduce trade tensions among the U.S. and other countries. I believe that this is the primary reason for investors rushing to buy gold than the current forecasts on economic growth that rely on things we do know about. After all, a trade war could make things much worse. I think that the market correctly assesses that retaliation is certain if Trump goes forward with his plans regarding tariffs. China's more lukewarm response may have something to do with the fact that the Trump administration "discriminated" against Canada and Mexico by announcing a 10% tariff on imports from China, rather than the more aggressive 25% for the two neighboring countries. Canada and Mexico retaliated in kind before they came to an agreement, but the tariffs were simply postponed. At the same time, the EU Commission has stated that it would react "firmly and immediately" against any tariff increases the Trump administration announces.

All of this fuels the uncertainty I mentioned, and as long as this "tariff" matter is present, the markets have no reason to change course. Which brings me to the fact that tariffs on Canada and Mexico have been postponed until March 4. That's less than two weeks from now. However, reciprocal tariffs may be coming as early as April 2. The gold price action is likely to have more room to continue its strong trend upward in the short term.

Technicals

Speaking of which, the technical picture both agrees with the fundamentals and shows us promising signs of an enduring trend. The price action has widened the distance between the current level and the 50-day moving average, indicating a strong demand; it's interesting to see how the 50-day moving average has played the role of a soft resistance level in the last months of 2024, but the pattern was broken last month.

As indicated by the Bollinger Band channel widening, volatility is rising, which is to be expected with such a strong trend. At the same time, the current price being that close to the upper band reflects the strong momentum.

Now, pay attention to this. The bands are set at 2.5 standard deviations, so only 1.2% of the price closes should occur outside the bands, making the multiple times that the price closed above the upper band lately a rare phenomenon. This also represents the very strong demand for gold right now.

As for the RSI being just above 70 right now, many traders may think that it means gold is overbought. Within the context of the fundamentals, it actually indicates high momentum. The RSI doesn't have much predictive value for trend reversals, in my opinion. I think it's more reasonable to use it to simply assess the momentum because if you examine the chart above, the RSI has managed to cross 70 and stay meaningfully above it for some time. Last week it went down, below 70, and now it's back above that level again.

Risks

So, the primary risks come down to fundamentals. Because gold is something that I believe all diversified portfolios should have to some extent as a hedge against market volatility and economic uncertainty such as today, I will have to talk about opportunity risks. The long-term case for gold is after all strong but it was beyond the scope of this article.

First of all, since there's good reason to believe that the current price action is more of a result of trade policy uncertainty rather than monetary policy because of the magnitude of the price increase, the timeline, and how other assets have behaved, you should keep an eye on that factor.

Trump is relatively unpredictable as president and I have my reservations regarding the extent to which tariff threats are more bark than bite; put it another way, some policies have the end goal of a non-economic outcome but others (like reciprocal tariffs) may be more bite than bark. The markets price in the uncertainty but should price it out if said uncertainty is not replaced with actions as bold as the "threats" in regard to tariffs. Maybe the tariffs on Canadian and Mexican imports will be "postponed indefinitely" next month. Maybe reciprocal tariffs will not ignite a trade war and instead manage to bring tariffs on U.S. exports down. Maybe tariffs on aluminum and steel don't have to be followed by tariffs on gold (which is probably a key factor when it comes to the price action right now).

Moreover, because there is a connection between tariffs and a higher risk of higher inflation, there is a good reason to believe that essentially the market is concerned about higher inflation as a result of tariffs. So, bear in mind that more positive inflation expectations as a result of data that prove the current uptrend of CPI to be less threatening could result in a pullback that would be independent of the potential impact of tariffs.

All of these factors could change the current trend of gold outperforming other assets, and a tilt to gold could result in an opportunity cost.

These are all matters you should monitor, and I will do my best to keep up with the important changes regarding the outlook for gold and write relevant articles in the coming weeks.

For now, I look forward to your comments and having a discussion about the outlook for gold with you. By the way, I have no problem talking about politics but I think that such a discussion should be in the context of trading and investing.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Foreign Direct Investment Increased To A Record $41 Trillion
data.description:
Global foreign direct investment grew again in 2023 after declining the previous year. Inward direct investment climbed $1.75 trillion, or 4.4 percent, reaching a record $41 trillion, according to the IMF's latest Coordinated Direct Investment Survey, which provides detailed information on direct investment positions between countries.

FDI rose in most regions, with Central and South Asia, Europe, and North and Central America contributing most. Direct investment between advanced economies grew by $880 billion, or 3.6 percent, while those from advanced economies to emerging market and developing economies rose by $538 billion, or 7.6 percent.

As our Chart of the Week shows, the United States extended its lead as the top destination for direct investment. Singapore recorded the largest gain in 2023, with its position rising $307 billion, followed by $227 billion for the United States and $164 billion for Germany. Meanwhile, the Netherlands and Luxembourg posted the steepest declines but remained in the top five, alongside the United States, China, and the United Kingdom.

The United States continues to lead as the top destination for inward foreign direct investment

Top 10 FDI recipients, billions of USD

Strong growth was also seen in many emerging economies. Most notably, India, Mexico, and Brazil each saw their inward direct investment positions rise by around $130 billion or about 20 percent, marking the largest increase for these three economies in total since the survey began in 2009.
